name: Patient I
presenting_complaint: Severe sore throat and difficulty swallowing since yesterday evening, now drooling and unable to lie flat.
history: Type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2), former smoker. NRS 8/10 throat pain. Muffled 'hot potato' voice. Sitting forward. AVPU=A. Adult son is at the bedside, agitated and demanding antibiotics now, repeatedly interrupts the assessment and tells her not to consent to 'any tube down her throat.'

trajectory.state: Tripod posture, drooling, soft stridor when crying. Refuses to lie back. SpO2 97% upright.
Son blocks the door, demands oral antibiotics and discharge. De-escalation needed before any airway examination is possible. ENT and anaesthetics must be paged early.
Lateral soft tissue neck X-ray (or fibreoptic laryngoscopy in OR) shows swollen epiglottis. IV ceftriaxone + dexamethasone started. ENT, anaesthetics, ICU all engaged for double-set-up airway plan.

If managed in a controlled OR setting with ENT/anaesthetics: airway secured electively, ICU admission, good outcome. If forced examination on a struggling patient: laryngospasm and emergency cricothyroidotomy.

confirmatory_tests: Lateral soft tissue neck X-ray (thumbprint sign)
red_herrings: Son's pressure to discharge with oral antibiotics
SpO2 reads 97% — does not capture impending airway loss
